does: This paper identifies and addresses a critical failure mode in self-play red teaming for AI safety. The authors demonstrate that when the same model serves as both attacker and defender with shared parameters, the training dynamics collapse to self-consistency rather than genuine adversarial interaction, allowing the defender to trivially satisfy safety constraints without learning robust defenses. They propose Anchored Bipolicy Self-Play, which freezes the base model and trains separate low-rank adapters (LoRAs) for attacker and defender roles. This architectural change decouples the policy updates, maintaining adversarial pressure throughout training and reaching Nash equilibria that enforce substantive safety behaviors rather than degenerate solutions like always-refusing.
connects: This work extends the line of research using self-play and game-theoretic approaches for AI safety alignment, building on prior work that leverages zero-sum games between competing model instances. The paper directly responds to limitations in parameter-sharing schemes used in earlier self-play red teaming methods, which were adopted for stability but introduced theoretical constraints. It connects to broader work on LoRA-based efficient fine-tuning and architectural solutions for multi-agent training, while engaging with game theory concepts around Nash equilibria convergence. The paper also relates to research on robustness verification and adversarial testing of language models, proposing a principled game-theoretic alternative to standard red-teaming practices.
enables: This work opens pathways for more rigorous safety evaluation of large language models through properly incentivized adversarial training. By establishing that decoupled policies can maintain meaningful adversarial pressure, it enables development of safety benchmarks grounded in game-theoretic guarantees rather than heuristic red-teaming. The approach suggests broader applicability of bipolicy training to other alignment problems requiring robust adversarial interaction. Future work could explore convergence analysis of the proposed method, scaling to larger models, integration with other safety techniques like RLHF, and extension to multi-agent or asymmetric game settings. The method also potentially informs how to design training procedures that maintain genuine competitive pressure in cooperative-competitive hybrid settings.
